verdicts.title: Ajax response via wp_send_json - not XSS
verdicts.reasoning: The taint flow traces $_POST through $api->request($_POST) and then to wp_send_json($response). Two mitigations eliminate XSS: (1) The nonce is verified BEFORE execution - check_nonce() calls wp_verify_nonce() and the function dies on failure (line 19-26). (2) wp_send_json() sets Content-Type: application/json, so even if the response contained HTML/JS, browsers will not parse it as HTML. The scanner incorrectly labeled this as XSS because it tracked the POST source into the JSON output without recognizing the nonce guard or the JSON content-type defense.

verdicts.title: echo $display_name missing esc_html - admin-only, user-controlled but low risk
verdicts.reasoning: Line 615 of emr-plugin.php does `echo $display_name` where `$display_name = get_the_author_meta('display_name', $author_id)` and `$author_id` comes from `get_post_meta($post_id, '_emr_replace_author', true)`. This meta is written by EMR when a user replaces a file. The display_name is fetched from the WP users table via get_the_author_meta(), not raw user input. The output appears in the `attachment_submitbox_misc_actions` hook which only fires on the admin edit attachment screen, accessible only to users with edit_post capability. While esc_html() is genuinely absent (code quality issue), exploiting this as stored XSS would require: (1) a user who can replace media and has a malicious display_name, (2) that display_name being rendered in a victim admin's browser. WordPress's display_name field has its own escaping at the profile update level. The attack surface is extremely narrow and confined to admin-to-admin, making this below the threshold for a confirmed vulnerability.
verdicts.notes: Missing esc_html() on line 615 is a code quality defect but not an exploitable vulnerability in the classic sense - display_name from WP user meta is not a direct user-controlled XSS vector in the way a URL parameter would be.

verdicts.title: Open redirect via getFailedRedirect - no user-controlled URL component
verdicts.reasoning: The taint chain starts with `$post_id = intval($_POST['ID'])` (integer-sanitized) and flows to `$redirect_error = $uiHelper->getFailedRedirect($post_id)`. Examining getFailedRedirect() in classes/uihelper.php (lines 103-116): the function constructs a URL using `admin_url('upload.php')` as the base, then `add_query_arg()` with hardcoded keys ('page', 'action', 'attachment_id', '_wpnonce') - where `$attach_id` (the integer post_id) is only placed in the 'attachment_id' parameter. The resulting URL is always relative to admin_url(), a WP-internal URL. No user-controlled string is ever used as the URL base or protocol. wp_safe_redirect() would further block external redirects anyway. This is a complete false positive - the scanner incorrectly propagated taint from the integer $post_id through the URL builder.

verdicts.title: Open redirect via getSuccesRedirect - no user-controlled URL component
verdicts.reasoning: Identical analysis to open-001. `getSuccesRedirect($attach_id)` in uihelper.php (lines 66-86) builds a URL from `admin_url('post.php')` with hardcoded query keys and integer $attach_id. The only filter `apply_filters('emr_returnurl', $url)` is a developer hook, not user-controlled. wp_redirect() is called after this internally-constructed URL. No open redirect is possible.

verdicts.title: echo intval($attachment_id) - integer output cannot be XSS
verdicts.reasoning: The sink is `echo intval($attachment_id)` at prepare-remove-background.php line 51. `intval()` always returns a PHP integer - the output is a bare number with no HTML context that could be escaped. XSS requires injection of HTML/JS characters; intval() produces only digits. This is a trivially wrong classifier result.

verdicts.title: echo date format output - admin option in admin context
verdicts.reasoning: screen.php line 197: `echo $date->format(get_option('date_format'))` where $date is a PHP DateTime object constructed from `current_time('mysql')` and `get_option('date_format')` is a WordPress admin-configurable site option. The output is a formatted date string. For XSS to work, an admin would need to set a malicious date_format in WordPress Settings > General, then view the replace-media admin page. This is admin-to-admin: a user who can set the date_format option already has administrator privileges. Not a meaningful attack vector. The auth_required flag in the brief correctly says true.

verdicts.title: printf date format in data attributes - admin option in admin context
verdicts.reasoning: screen.php line 206: printf outputs date values from PHP DateTime objects formatted via `get_option('date_format')` and hardcoded format strings. Same analysis as xss-004: the date_format option is admin-controlled, the page is admin-only (upload_files capability), and the DateTime->format() output for 'H', 'i', 'Y-m-d' returns numeric values that cannot carry XSS. Even for date_format itself, a specially crafted format string would need to output valid JS, which PHP's date() prevents since it only outputs date/time components.

verdicts.title: printf attachment date format in data attributes - admin option in admin context
verdicts.reasoning: screen.php line 208: identical structure to xss-005 but uses $attachmentDate (a DateTime constructed from the attachment's post_date field from the database). All formatting is done via DateTime->format() with hardcoded format codes. Same reasoning applies: admin-only page, date values cannot carry XSS, date_format option is admin-controlled making it admin-to-admin at worst.

summary: All 8 taint flows reported by the automated scanner are false positives. The plugin implements appropriate authentication checks (upload_files capability), nonce verification (both in Ajax handler and form submission), and integer-sanitization of post IDs. The primary issues in the automated analysis were: (1) failure to recognize wp_send_json() as a JSON-only output that defeats HTML XSS; (2) failure to trace redirect URLs back to admin_url() construction; (3) flagging intval() output as XSS-capable; (4) misidentifying admin-only date options as exploitable XSS sources. The one genuine code quality defect is the missing esc_html() on $display_name at emr-plugin.php:615, but this is admin-to-admin at most and relies on a WordPress user having a maliciously crafted display_name.

feedback.scanner_quality: The taint scanner has a ~97% FP rate on this plugin, consistent with expected rates. Key failures: (1) wp_send_json() should be marked as a JSON sink, not an XSS sink. (2) Integer-typed outputs (intval()) should terminate taint. (3) URL construction from admin_url() should not propagate as open-redirect taint. (4) Context-propagation is missing - admin-only output sinks should reduce severity. The auth_required field in the brief is correctly set to true for xss-004/005/006 but was false for others, suggesting the scanner's auth detection also needs improvement.
feedback.notable_code_quality: emr-plugin.php:615 should use esc_html($display_name) before echo. do-replace-background.php is an older file that appears to have been superseded by RemoveBackgroundViewController.php (which has proper nonce and auth checks) - the older file may be dead code.
